elements.sent-2411-001: In this article, we investigate the bivariate \ml analysis of pairs of Borel probability measures.
elements.sent-2411-002: We prove that, contrarily to what happens in the univariate case, the natural extension of the Legendre spectrum does not yield an upper bound for the bivariate \ml spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-003: For this we build a pair of measures for which the two spectra have disjoint supports.
elements.sent-2411-004: Then we study the bivariate \ml behavior of an archetypical pair of randomly correlated measures, which give new, surprising, behaviors, enriching the narrow class of measures for which such an analysis is achieved.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our purpose here is to further investigate the multivariate multifractal analysis of measures by studying the validity of a multifractal formalism, and by illustrating the diversity of possible behaviors with the example of a pair of randomly correlated binomial Bernoulli cascades, which is the archetype of two correlated multifractal objects.
elements.sent-2411-006: Recall that the lower and upper local dimensions of a Borel measure $\mu$ defined on $\zu$ at $x$ are respectively defined as \begin{equation}\dimu (\mu,x)=\liminf_{r \to 0^+}\frac{\log \mu(B(x,r))}{ \log r} \  and  \ {\overline \dim}(\mu,x)=\limsup_{r\to 0^+}\frac{\log \mu(B(x,r))}{ \log r },\end{equation} and $\dim (\mu,x)$ stands for the common value of $\dimu (\mu,x)$ and $\dimo(\mu,x)$ when these quantities agree.
elements.sent-2411-007: Then, using the Hausdorff dimension $\dim$, the multifractal spectrum \begin{equation}\label{eq-defdmu} H\mapsto D_\mu(H) = \dim \underline E_\mu(H), where \underline E_\mu(H):=\left \{x\in[0,1] : {\underline \dim_\locloc}(\mu,x)=H\right \}\end{equation} describes the distribution of the singularities of $\mu$.
elements.sent-2411-008: We emphasize that the lower local dimension, defined for every point in the support of $\mu$, is considered here.
elements.sent-2411-009: An important quantity is the $L^q$-spectrum $\tau_{\mu}$ of a probability measure $\mu$ supported by $\zu$.
elements.sent-2411-010: Define $\mathcal{D _j $ as the set of dyadic intervals of generation $j\geq 0$, i.e. \begin{equation}D_j = \{[k2^{-j},(k+1)2^{-j}): k\in \{0,..., 2^1-1\}\},\end{equation} and $\mathcal{D =\bigcup_{j\geq 0}\mathcal{D _j$ the set of dyadic intervals of $\zu$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Then the $L^q$-spectrum $\tau_{\mu}$ is \begin{equation}\label{deftaumu} \tau_\mu(q) = \liminf_{j\to +\infty} \tau_{\mu,j}(q) , \ where \tau_{\mu,j}(q) = \frac{-1}{j} \log_2 \sum_{I\in \mathcal{D}_j} \mu(3I)^q,\end{equation} where for any interval $I$, $3I$ stands for the interval with same center as $I$ and radius three times that of $I$.
elements.sent-2411-012: It is standard that for Borel measures $\mu$ on $\zu$ (and in $\zu^d$), the multifractal spectrum $D_\mu$ is bounded above by the Legendre spectrum of the $L^q$-spectrum, i.e. one always has $D_\mu(H)\leq \tau_\mu^*(H) := \inf_{q\in \R (q H-\tau_\mu(q))$ \cite{FrischParisi,Halsey,BRMICHPEY .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: When the equality $D_\mu(H)= \tau_\mu^*(H)$ holds, $\mu$ is said to satisfy the multifractal formalism at $H$.
elements.sent-2411-014: The investigation of \ml properties of measures (and functions) is now a standard issue in fractal geometry, ergodic theory and geometric measure theory, and has been investigated for large classes of deterministic and random Borel probability measures.
elements.sent-2411-015: Among many examples, let us mention Gibbs measures invariant by various dynamical systems \cite{BRMICHPEY,BBP,FAN,FENG2007,BarralFeng2 , self-similar and self-affine measures \cite{LauNgai,FengLau,Barral-Feng-21 , Mandelbrot cascades \cite{BMPSPUM , Baire typical measures \cite{BuS2,Bay .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: This is still an active research field, with still open conjectures regarding the value of $D_\mu$ and the validity of the \ml formalism especially for self-similar and self-affine measures, and connexions with many mathematical fields.
elements.sent-2411-017: The validity of the multifractal formalism reflects a deep relationship between local behaviors and geometrical properties of the measure $\mu$ and global statistics (the $L^q$-spectrum) computed on $\mu$ at various scales.
elements.sent-2411-018: In addition, this key relationship allows the multifractal spectrum to be estimated.
elements.sent-2411-019: Indeed, while the \ml spectrum is not computable on (discretized, finite) data, several robust algorithms have been developed to estimate the $L^q$-spectrum, see \cite{Wendt2007d and the references therein for instance.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Then, when the \ml formalism holds, the \ml spectrum becomes accessible as the Legendre transform of the estimated $L^q$-spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-021: This \ml spectrum has proved to be a relevant quantity in signal and image processing, both as an analyzing and as a classification method.
elements.sent-2411-022: It is a widely used tool in many applications, for instance physics and geophysics, EEG and ECG analyses, urban data, ..
elements.sent-2411-023: Our purpose here is to consider the simultaneous \ml analysis of measures, which is referred to as multivariate \ml analysis.
elements.sent-2411-024: More precisely, for a family of measures $(\mu_i)_{i=1,...,n}$ supported on $\zu$, this consists first in considering the sets \begin{equation}\label{defemu1mu2} \underline E_{\mu_1,...,\mu_n}(H_1,...,H_n)=\{x\in \zu: \dimu (\mu_i,x)=H_i for all i\in\{1,...,n\}\}\end{equation} and then in computing the associated multivariate \ml spectrum \begin{equation}\label{defDmu1mu2} D_{\mu_1,...,\mu_n}(H_1,...,H_n)= \dim \underline E_{\mu_1,...,\mu_n}(H_1,...,H_n).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-025: Not only is it a natural question to analyze the $(\mu_i)$ simultaneously from a mathematical standpoint, but also it is a key issue in many applications where phenomena consist naturally of several signals/images.
elements.sent-2411-026: For instance, in neurosciences, EEG, scanners and MRI are built from several views of the same object; in geography, various measures and maps (population density, housing, revenues, altitude,...) are needed to fully describe an area or a city, An important challenge is then, on top of describing each set of data, to uncover their correlation and the way the influence each other.
elements.sent-2411-027: The multivariate \ml spectrum \eqref{defDmu1mu2 , and the multivariate $L^q$-spectrum associated with it, are relevant indicators to achieve such studies by unfolding existing correlations between fractal and \ml parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-028: This line of research is quite recent, even if there is already a collection of mathematical results in this area.
elements.sent-2411-029: This has been investigated in \cite{BarrSau,BarSauSchmel for two Gibbs measures $\mu$ and $\nu$ invariant by the same dynamical system: there, the estimation of the Hausdorff dimension of the sets $ \{ x\in \zu: \dim (\mu,x)=H_1,\dim(\nu,x)=H_2\}$ (where the limit local dimension is used) is achieved.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Such studies are more involved in general than the multifractal analysis of a single measure, for at least two reasons.
elements.sent-2411-031: First, since the level sets in \eqref{defemu1mu2 can be rewritten as $\underline E_{\mu_1,...,\mu_n (H_1,...,H_n) = \bigcap_{i=1 ^n \underline E_{\mu_i (H_i)$, performing the multivariate multifractal analysis amounts to investigating the size of intersections of (fractal-like) sets, which is known to be a delicate issue (see for instance Marstrand slicing theorems \cite{Marstrand54,Mattila75,Fal93,MATTILA or recent progresses on intersections of Cantor sets \cite{Shmerkin_annals,Wu_annals .}}}}}}
